nodes.label: User accounts, credits, and paid generation research
nodes.norm_label: user accounts, credits, and paid generation research

nodes.label: What other AI generation services do
nodes.norm_label: what other ai generation services do

nodes.label: Credits are the normal user-facing abstraction
nodes.norm_label: credits are the normal user-facing abstraction

nodes.label: Code Review (two-axis skill)
nodes.rationale: The two axes are kept separate so one cannot mask the other: code can pass every standard yet implement the wrong thing (Standards pass, Spec fail), or do exactly what the issue asked while breaking conventions (Spec pass, Standards fail). Findings are never merged or reranked across axes.

nodes.label: Depth (leverage at the interface)
nodes.rationale: Depth-as-leverage was chosen over Ousterhout's implementation-lines-to-interface-lines ratio because the ratio rewards padding the implementation.

nodes.label: Dependency Categories (in-process, local-substitutable, remote-owned, true external)
nodes.rationale: The dependency category determines how the deepened module is tested across its seam: in-process needs no adapter; local-substitutable uses test stand-ins like PGLite; remote-but-owned uses ports & adapters; true external takes an injected port with a mock adapter.
nodes.norm_label: dependency categories (in-process, local-substitutable, remote-owned, true external)

nodes.label: Seam Discipline
nodes.rationale: One adapter means a hypothetical seam, two adapters means a real one — a single-adapter seam is just indirection. Internal seams must not be exposed through the interface just because tests use them.

nodes.label: Replace, Don't Layer (testing strategy)
nodes.rationale: Old unit tests on shallow modules become waste once tests at the deepened module's interface exist — delete them. Tests describe behaviour, not implementation; a test that must change when the implementation changes is testing past the interface.

nodes.label: Design It Twice (parallel sub-agent pattern)
nodes.rationale: Based on Ousterhout: your first idea is unlikely to be the best. 3+ parallel sub-agents each produce a radically different interface under a distinct design constraint (minimal, flexible, common-caller-optimised, ports-and-adapters), then designs are compared on depth, locality, and seam placement with an opinionated recommendation.

nodes.label: Regression Test at a Correct Seam
nodes.rationale: Write the regression test before the fix, but only at a seam that exercises the real bug pattern as it occurs at the call site — a too-shallow seam gives false confidence. If no correct seam exists, that itself is the finding: the architecture is preventing the bug from being locked down, and it hands off to /improve-codebase-architecture.
nodes.norm_label: regression test at a correct seam

nodes.label: Domain Modeling (skill)
nodes.rationale: The active discipline of changing the domain model — challenging terms, sharpening fuzzy language, stress-testing scenarios, cross-referencing code — as distinct from merely reading CONTEXT.md for vocabulary, which any skill can do.

nodes.label: CONTEXT.md (domain glossary)
nodes.rationale: A glossary and nothing else — opinionated canonical terms with tight one-to-two-sentence definitions and explicit Avoid lists; totally devoid of implementation details; only terms specific to the project's context belong. Created lazily when the first term is resolved.

nodes.label: Grilling (relentless interview primitive)
nodes.rationale: Ask questions one at a time with a recommended answer each — multiple questions at once is bewildering. Explore the codebase instead of asking when possible; never enact the plan until shared understanding is confirmed.

nodes.label: Handoff (compact conversation into document)
nodes.rationale: Bridges context windows: compacts the conversation into a temp-directory markdown file a fresh session references. Does not duplicate content already in PRDs, plans, ADRs, issues, commits, or diffs — references them by path or URL; redacts sensitive information.

nodes.label: Deepening Opportunity
nodes.rationale: A refactor that turns shallow modules into deep ones; the aim is testability and AI-navigability. Candidates contradicting an existing ADR are only surfaced when the friction is real enough to warrant reopening the ADR.

nodes.label: Architecture Review HTML Report Format
nodes.rationale: A single self-contained HTML file in the OS temp dir; diagrams carry the weight and prose is sparse. Mermaid handles graph-shaped diagrams, hand-built divs/SVG handle editorial visuals (mass diagrams, cross-sections) — leaning on Mermaid for everything looks generic. Tone uses the /codebase-design glossary exactly, never 'component', 'service', or 'boundary'.

nodes.label: Prototype (throwaway code answering a question)
nodes.rationale: A prototype is throwaway code that answers one question; the question decides the shape (logic TUI vs UI variants). Rules: throwaway from day one and clearly marked, one command to run, no persistence, skip the polish, surface the state, and delete or absorb when done — the answer is the only thing worth keeping.

nodes.label: Logic Prototype (interactive terminal state driver)
nodes.rationale: For questions about business logic, state transitions, or data shape — things that look reasonable on paper but only feel wrong once pushed through real cases. A lightweight TUI clears and re-renders the full frame per action so the user always sees one stable view.

nodes.label: UI Prototype (radically different variants on one route)
nodes.rationale: Variants are judged best butting up against the rest of the app (sub-shape A, existing page, preferred) because a throwaway route is a vacuum where every variant looks fine in isolation. Variants must be structurally different — different layout, hierarchy, primary affordance — not just different colours.
nodes.norm_label: ui prototype (radically different variants on one route)

nodes.label: Floating Variant Switcher
nodes.rationale: A fixed bottom-centre pill cycling variants via a ?variant= URL search param so variants are shareable and reload-stable; visually distinct from the design under evaluation and hidden in production builds so a stray prototype merge cannot ship it.

nodes.label: Research (background primary-source investigation)
nodes.rationale: A background agent investigates against primary sources — official docs, source code, specs, first-party APIs, never a secondary write-up — and leaves a cited Markdown file in the repo so the main session keeps working while it reads.

nodes.label: Setup Matt Pocock's Skills (per-repo configuration)
nodes.rationale: Prompt-driven, not a deterministic script: explore the repo, present findings, walk the user through three decisions one at a time (issue tracker, triage labels, domain docs), confirm, then write docs/agents/*.md and an Agent skills block. Never creates AGENTS.md when CLAUDE.md exists or vice versa.

nodes.label: Triage Label Vocabulary (five canonical roles)
nodes.rationale: needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix — a state machine the /triage skill drives. Repos with existing label names map them here so the skill applies the right strings instead of creating duplicates.

nodes.label: Local Markdown Issue Tracker Seed Template
nodes.norm_label: local markdown issue tracker seed template

nodes.label: Domain Docs Consumer Rules
nodes.rationale: Skills read CONTEXT.md / CONTEXT-MAP.md and docs/adr/ before exploring, proceed silently if absent (files are created lazily by /domain-modeling), use the glossary's vocabulary without drifting to avoided synonyms, and surface ADR conflicts explicitly rather than silently overriding.

nodes.label: Wayfinding Operations (map issue, child tickets, blocking, frontier, claim, resolve)
nodes.rationale: The map is a single issue labelled wayfinder:map with child sub-issues as tickets; blocking uses GitHub's native issue dependencies (blocked_by with the numeric database id, not the #number or node_id), with a 'Blocked by:' body line as fallback. Frontier = first unblocked, unassigned open child in map order.
nodes.norm_label: wayfinding operations (map issue, child tickets, blocking, frontier, claim, resolve)

nodes.label: Canonical Triage Roles
nodes.rationale: Skills speak in five canonical role names (needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix) mapped to repo-specific label strings so the skills stay tracker-agnostic.

nodes.label: Wayfinding Operations (GitLab)
nodes.rationale: Map/child-ticket/blocking/frontier/claim/resolve contract implemented with GitLab issues, labels, and native blocking links; falls back to a Blocked-by description line on free tiers.

nodes.label: Wayfinding Operations (Local Markdown)
nodes.rationale: Same map/child-ticket/blocking/frontier/claim/resolve contract implemented as markdown files under .scratch/ with Status: and Blocked by: lines.

nodes.label: Test Seam
nodes.rationale: Tests live only at pre-agreed public seams, confirmed with the user first, so testing effort lands on critical paths instead of every edge case.

nodes.label: Vertical Slice (Tracer Bullet)
nodes.rationale: One test, one implementation, repeat; avoids horizontal slicing where bulk tests verify imagined behavior and commit to test structure before the implementation is understood.

nodes.label: TDD Anti-Patterns
nodes.rationale: Three named failures: implementation-coupled tests, tautological tests, and horizontal slicing.

nodes.label: System Boundary Mocking
nodes.rationale: Mock only external APIs, databases, time/randomness, and filesystem; never your own classes or internal collaborators, so tests survive refactors.

nodes.label: SDK-Style Interfaces
nodes.rationale: Specific functions per external operation instead of one generic fetcher: each mock returns one shape, no conditional logic in test setup, type safety per endpoint.

nodes.label: Behavioral (Integration-Style) Testing
nodes.rationale: Tests use public API only, describe WHAT not HOW, verify through the interface (not the database), and survive internal refactors.

nodes.label: Tautological Test
nodes.rationale: Expected value is recomputed the way the code computes it, so the test passes by construction; expected values must come from an independent source of truth.

nodes.label: Opinionated Glossary
nodes.rationale: Terms are added only once understood (compressed knowledge, not a dictionary), definitions stay tight, one best word is chosen with aliases listed as Avoid, and glossary terms are reused inside other definitions.

nodes.label: Learning Record
nodes.rationale: Teaching equivalent of ADRs: decision-grade insights (demonstrated understanding, disclosed prior knowledge, corrected misconceptions, mission shifts), never a session journal; superseded records are marked, not deleted, because the history of understanding is signal.

nodes.label: Learning Mission
nodes.rationale: The concrete real-world reason the user is learning; every teaching decision traces back to it. Concrete over abstract, one mission per workspace, revised when reality shifts, kept short enough to remain a compass rather than a plan.

nodes.label: Teaching Workspace
nodes.rationale: Stateful multi-session learning captured as files in the current directory: MISSION.md, RESOURCES.md, reference/, learning-records/, lessons/, assets/, NOTES.md.

nodes.label: Tracer Bullet Issue (Vertical Slice)
nodes.rationale: Each issue is a thin vertical slice through ALL integration layers (schema, API, UI, tests), demoable on its own, never a horizontal layer slice; published in dependency order with blockers first; file paths and code snippets are avoided because they go stale fast.

nodes.label: PRD Template
nodes.rationale: Synthesizes the current conversation (no interview) into Problem/Solution/User Stories/Implementation Decisions/Testing Decisions/Out of Scope; excludes file paths and code snippets because they go stale, except decision-rich prototype snippets; the ideal number of test seams is one, at the highest point possible.

nodes.label: Agent Brief
nodes.rationale: The authoritative spec an AFK agent works from. Durability over precision: describe interfaces, types, and behavioral contracts, never file paths or line numbers, because the issue may sit for weeks while the codebase changes. Behavioral not procedural, complete acceptance criteria, explicit out-of-scope boundaries.

nodes.label: Out-of-Scope Knowledge Base
nodes.rationale: Persistent records of rejected enhancements in .out-of-scope/, one file per concept, for institutional memory and dedup by concept similarity. Only rejected enhancements go here; already-implemented wontfix closures are excluded to avoid poisoning dedup checks with false rejections.

nodes.label: Model-Invoked Skill
nodes.rationale: Keeps its description so the agent (and other skills) can fire it; pays permanent context load in exchange for discoverability.

nodes.label: User-Invoked Skill
nodes.rationale: Description stripped: zero context load, but only the human can reach it, and no other skill can fire it; spends cognitive load — the human is the index.

nodes.label: Context Pointer
nodes.rationale: A reference in context naming out-of-context material and the condition for reaching it; the wording, not the target, decides when and how reliably the agent reaches — a weak pointer to must-have material is a variance bug.

nodes.label: Cognitive Load
nodes.rationale: The price of human agency, not a cost to minimise: spend it where human judgement matters, remove it where it does not.

nodes.label: Information Hierarchy
nodes.rationale: Content ranked by how immediately the agent needs it: in-file steps, in-file reference, disclosed reference behind a context pointer; in-file reference that should be disclosed buries the steps and turns attending to them into a coin-flip.

nodes.label: Progressive Disclosure
nodes.rationale: Moving reference behind a context pointer to protect the information hierarchy, not primarily to save tokens; licensed by branching — disclose what only some branches need, inline what every path needs.

nodes.label: Leading Word (Leitwort)
nodes.rationale: A compact pretrained concept (lesson, fog of war, tracer bullets) the agent thinks with; encodes a behavioural principle in minimal tokens by recruiting existing priors. Anchors execution in the body and invocation in the description.

nodes.label: Completion Criterion
nodes.rationale: Two lever properties: clarity resists premature completion; demand sets legwork — and demand binds flat reference too, which is how a stepless skill still carries an exhaustiveness bar.

nodes.label: Premature Completion
nodes.rationale: Ending a step before it is done because attention slips to being done. Defence in order: sharpen the completion criterion first (cheap, local); hide post-completion steps only when the criterion is irreducibly fuzzy and the rush is observed — and hiding only works across a real context boundary.

nodes.label: No-Op Instruction
nodes.rationale: An instruction the model already obeys by default; the test is model-relative — settled by running the skill, not by debate. Also the grading test for whether a leading word earns its repetitions.

nodes.label: Seam (Feathers)
nodes.rationale: A place where behaviour can be altered without editing in that place; where a module's interface lives. 'Boundary' is rejected as overloaded with DDD's bounded context. One adapter means a hypothetical seam; two adapters means a real one.

nodes.label: Adapter
nodes.rationale: A concrete thing satisfying an interface at a seam; describes role, not substance — a small adapter can have a large implementation (Postgres repo) or a large adapter a small one (in-memory fake).

nodes.label: Interface Is the Test Surface
nodes.rationale: Callers and tests cross the same seam; wanting to test past the interface means the module is probably the wrong shape.

nodes.label: Dependency Categories for Deepening
nodes.rationale: Four categories determine how a deepened module is tested across its seam: in-process (always deepenable), local-substitutable (test stand-in like PGLite), remote-but-owned (ports & adapters), true external (injected port with mock adapter).

nodes.label: Ports & Adapters
nodes.rationale: For owned remote dependencies: define a port at the seam, inject the transport as an adapter — in-memory for tests, HTTP/gRPC/queue for production — so the logic sits in one deep module even though deployed across a network.

nodes.label: Design It Twice (Ousterhout)
nodes.rationale: Your first idea is unlikely to be the best: spawn 3+ parallel sub-agents, each with a radically different design constraint (minimize interface, maximise flexibility, optimise common caller, ports & adapters), then compare on depth, locality, and seam placement and give an opinionated recommendation.

nodes.label: Tight Feedback Loop
nodes.rationale: A tight, red-capable, deterministic, fast, agent-runnable pass/fail signal is treated as 90% of debugging; hypothesising without one is the exact failure the skill prevents. Loops must be tightened (faster, sharper, deterministic) and non-deterministic bugs made debuggable by raising reproduction rate.

nodes.label: Minimised Repro
nodes.rationale: Shrinking the repro until every remaining element is load-bearing shrinks the Phase 3 hypothesis space and becomes the clean Phase 5 regression test.

nodes.label: Ranked Falsifiable Hypotheses
nodes.rationale: Generate 3-5 ranked hypotheses before testing any, each with a stated falsifiable prediction, because single-hypothesis generation anchors on the first plausible idea; showing the ranked list to the user is a cheap checkpoint.

nodes.label: CONTEXT.md Domain Glossary
nodes.rationale: An opinionated glossary at the repo root: pick one canonical term per concept and list synonyms under Avoid; tight one-to-two-sentence definitions; only context-specific terms, never general programming concepts or implementation details; created lazily when the first term is resolved.

nodes.label: CONTEXT-MAP.md Multi-Context Map
nodes.rationale: For multi-context repos, a root CONTEXT-MAP.md lists contexts, where each CONTEXT.md lives, and inter-context relationships (event flows, shared types); its presence signals a multi-context repo.

nodes.label: Grilling Skill
nodes.rationale: Relentless one-question-at-a-time interview to reach shared understanding of a plan; multiple questions at once is bewildering; codebase-answerable questions get explored instead of asked; no enactment until the user confirms.

nodes.label: Handoff Skill
nodes.rationale: Compacts the conversation into a handoff doc saved to the OS temp dir (not the workspace), with a suggested-skills section, redacted secrets, and references to existing artifacts (PRDs, plans, ADRs, issues) by path/URL instead of duplication.

nodes.label: Architecture HTML Report Format
nodes.rationale: Self-contained HTML in the OS temp dir; Tailwind + Mermaid via CDN; Mermaid for graph-shaped diagrams, hand-built divs/SVG for editorial visuals (mass diagrams, cross-sections) because leaning on Mermaid for everything looks generic; strict glossary tone — module/interface/seam/deep/shallow, never component/service/API/boundary.

nodes.label: Before/After Deepening Diagram
nodes.rationale: The centrepiece of each candidate card: side-by-side before/after visualisation using varied patterns (Mermaid graph, hand-built boxes-and-arrows, cross-section, mass diagram, call-graph collapse); if the diagram needs a paragraph to be understood, redraw the diagram.

nodes.label: Deepening Opportunity
nodes.rationale: A refactor that turns shallow modules (interface nearly as complex as implementation) into deep ones; the aim is testability and AI-navigability, judged in terms of locality and leverage.

nodes.label: Deletion Test
nodes.rationale: Applied to suspected-shallow modules: would deleting it concentrate complexity, or just move it? 'Yes, concentrates' is the signal a deepening candidate is real. Principle originates in the /codebase-design skill.

nodes.label: Portable Pure Logic Module
nodes.rationale: The logic under question (reducer, state machine, pure functions, or stateful class) sits behind a small pure interface with no I/O or terminal code, so the validated logic can be lifted into the real codebase while the TUI shell is deleted.

nodes.label: Lightweight TUI Frame Loop
nodes.rationale: Clear-screen-and-re-render on every action so the user sees one stable frame (state pretty-printed, then keyboard shortcuts) instead of growing scrollback; the throwaway shell that drives the pure logic module by hand.

nodes.label: Throwaway Prototype
nodes.rationale: Throwaway code that answers one design question; the question decides the shape (logic TUI vs UI variants). Rules: clearly marked throwaway, one command to run, no persistence, no polish, surface the state, and delete or absorb when done — only the answer is worth keeping.

nodes.label: URL-Param Variant Switcher
nodes.rationale: Several structurally different UI variants on one route gated by a ?variant= search param, cycled via a floating bottom bar (arrow keys, shareable URL, hidden in production). Prefer hosting variants inside an existing page: an isolated route is a vacuum where every variant looks fine.

nodes.label: Primary-Source Research
nodes.rationale: A background agent investigates against primary sources only (official docs, source code, specs, first-party APIs), follows every claim back to the source that owns it, and writes cited findings to a Markdown file matching the repo's note conventions.

nodes.label: Setup Matt Pocock Skills
nodes.rationale: One-time prompt-driven scaffolding of per-repo config the engineering skills assume: issue tracker choice (GitHub/GitLab/local markdown/other), triage label vocabulary mapping, and domain-doc layout (single vs multi context); writes docs/agents/*.md and an Agent skills block into whichever of CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md already exists.

nodes.label: Domain Docs Consumer Rules
nodes.rationale: Skills read CONTEXT.md (or CONTEXT-MAP.md) and docs/adr/ before exploring, proceed silently if absent (files are created lazily by /domain-modeling), use the glossary's vocabulary in all output, and flag ADR conflicts explicitly rather than silently overriding.

nodes.label: GitHub Issue Tracker Conventions
nodes.rationale: Issues and PRDs live as GitHub issues operated via the gh CLI; a PRs-as-request-surface flag routes external contributor PRs through the same triage labels; blocking uses GitHub native issue dependencies with a Blocked-by body-line fallback.

nodes.label: Wayfinding Operations
nodes.rationale: The tracker-agnostic contract /wayfinder relies on: a map (issue/file with Notes, Decisions-so-far, Fog), child tickets typed research/prototype/grilling/task, blocking edges, a frontier query (open, unblocked, unclaimed, first in map order), claim as first write, and resolve (answer, close, append context pointer to the map).

nodes.label: GitLab Issue Tracker Conventions
nodes.rationale: Same contract as the GitHub backend but via the glab CLI; GitLab calls comments notes and PRs merge requests; native blocking links are Premium/Ultimate-only so the free tier falls back to a Blocked-by description line.

nodes.label: Local Markdown Issue Tracker Conventions
nodes.rationale: Issues and PRDs live as markdown under .scratch/<feature>/ with numbered issue files; triage state is a Status: line and comments append under a Comments heading — good for solo projects or repos without a remote.

nodes.label: Five Canonical Triage Roles
nodes.rationale: needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix — a triage state machine (evaluate, waiting on reporter, AFK-agent-ready, human-required, won't fix). Skills speak in roles; the mapping table lets repos keep their own label strings without the skill creating duplicates.

nodes.label: Seam (Test Boundary)
nodes.rationale: The public boundary where behavior is observed without reaching inside; tests live only at seams pre-agreed with the user, so testing effort lands on critical paths and complex logic instead of every edge case.

nodes.label: Red-Green Loop
nodes.rationale: Write the failing test first, then only enough code to pass it — one seam, one test, one minimal implementation per cycle; refactoring is deliberately excluded from the loop and deferred to the code-review stage.

nodes.label: Vertical Slices (Tracer Bullets)
nodes.rationale: One test then one implementation, repeated — versus horizontal slicing (all tests first) which verifies imagined behavior, tests shape rather than user-facing behavior, and commits to test structure before the implementation is understood.

nodes.label: Mock at System Boundaries Only
nodes.rationale: Mock external APIs, time/randomness, sometimes DBs and filesystem — never your own classes, internal collaborators, or anything you control, because mocking internals couples tests to implementation.

nodes.label: Dependency Injection for Mockability
nodes.rationale: Pass external dependencies in rather than constructing them internally (e.g. paymentClient parameter vs new StripeClient inside), so boundaries can be swapped in tests.

nodes.label: SDK-Style Interfaces over Generic Fetchers
nodes.rationale: One specific function per external operation instead of a generic fetch wrapper: each mock returns one shape, no conditional logic in test setup, per-endpoint type safety, and tests reveal which endpoints they exercise.

nodes.label: Integration-Style Tests
nodes.rationale: Test observable behavior through the public API only, so tests describe WHAT not HOW and survive internal refactors. Mocking internal collaborators or verifying through external means (e.g. raw DB queries) couples tests to implementation details.

nodes.label: Tautological Tests
nodes.rationale: Failure mode where the expected value restates the implementation (recomputed the same way the code computes it), so the test passes by construction. Cure: assert against an independent known literal.

nodes.label: Teach GLOSSARY.md Format
nodes.rationale: The glossary is a record of compressed knowledge, not a dictionary to learn from: a term is added only when the user understands it, because compressing a concept into a tight definition is itself evidence of understanding.

nodes.label: Learning Record
nodes.rationale: The teaching equivalent of ADRs: captures non-obvious lessons, disclosed prior knowledge, and corrected misconceptions — decision-grade insights, not a session journal. Coverage is not learning; records require evidence. Superseded records are marked, not deleted, because the history of how understanding evolved is itself signal.

nodes.label: MISSION.md Format
nodes.rationale: One mission per workspace, concrete over abstract, because every teaching decision must trace back to the user's real-world goal. A bad mission is worse than no mission; if it runs past a screen it has stopped being a compass and started being a plan.

nodes.label: RESOURCES.md Format
nodes.rationale: Curated high-trust sources grouped Knowledge vs Wisdom, because explainer knowledge must be drawn from here rather than parametric guesses. Prune ruthlessly: five sharp sources beat thirty mediocre ones; gaps are surfaced explicitly to drive future search.

nodes.label: Lesson
nodes.rationale: The primary unit of teaching: one self-contained, beautiful HTML file teaching one tightly-scoped thing tied to the mission. Kept short because learners' working memory is small; each lesson delivers a single tangible win inside the zone of proximal development, built from reusable components in ./assets/.

nodes.label: Zone of Proximal Development
nodes.rationale: The user should feel challenged 'just enough' each lesson. Calculated from learning records plus the mission — teach the most relevant thing that fits inside it.

nodes.label: Fluency vs Storage Strength
nodes.rationale: Fluency (in-the-moment retrieval) gives an illusory sense of mastery; storage strength (long-term retention) is the real goal. Built via desirable difficulty: retrieval practice, spacing, and interleaving. For knowledge acquisition difficulty is the enemy; for skill acquisition it is the tool.

nodes.label: Knowledge, Skills, Wisdom Triad
nodes.rationale: Deep learning needs knowledge (from high-trust resources, never parametric guesses), skills (from interactive lessons with tight feedback loops), and wisdom (from real-world communities of practitioners). Wisdom questions are answered but ultimately delegated to a community.

nodes.label: Tracer Bullet Vertical Slice
nodes.rationale: Each issue is a thin vertical slice cutting through ALL integration layers end-to-end (schema, API, UI, tests) rather than a horizontal slice of one layer, so a completed slice is demoable or verifiable on its own. Prefactoring first: make the change easy, then make the easy change.

nodes.label: To PRD Skill
nodes.rationale: Synthesizes the current conversation into a PRD without interviewing the user. Test seams are sketched first: prefer existing seams, use the highest seam possible, and minimize seam count — the ideal number is one. File paths and code snippets are excluded because they go stale fast; only prototype-derived decision-rich snippets (state machines, schemas, type shapes) are inlined.

nodes.label: Agent Brief
nodes.rationale: The authoritative contract an AFK agent works from. Durability over precision: describe interfaces, types, and behavioral contracts, never file paths or line numbers, because the issue may sit for weeks while the codebase changes. Behavioral not procedural (what, not how), with concrete testable acceptance criteria and explicit out-of-scope boundaries to prevent gold-plating.

nodes.label: Out-of-Scope Knowledge Base
nodes.rationale: Persistent .out-of-scope/ records of rejected feature requests, serving institutional memory and deduplication (matching by concept similarity, not keyword). One file per concept, not per issue. Only rejected enhancements go here — never already-implemented wontfixes, which would poison the dedup checks with false rejections. Reasons must be durable, not temporary circumstances.

nodes.label: Triage State Machine
nodes.rationale: Two category roles (bug, enhancement) and five state roles (needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix); every triaged issue carries exactly one of each. A PR is treated as an issue with attached code — same roles, same states, same machine. Claims are verified (reproduce the bug, run the PR's tests) before grilling, because a confirmed verification makes a much stronger agent brief.

nodes.label: Wayfinder Shared Map
nodes.rationale: A single tracker issue (label wayfinder:map) whose tickets are child issues. The map is an index, not a store: a decision lives in exactly one place — its ticket — so the map only gists and links. Tickets are sized to one 100K-token agent session, claimed by assignment before any work, and never more than one is resolved per session.

nodes.label: Fog of War
nodes.rationale: The map is deliberately incomplete: don't chart what you can't yet see. The fog/ticket test is whether the question can be stated precisely now — not whether it can be answered now. Resolving a ticket clears fog ahead of it, graduating newly-specifiable questions into fresh tickets.

nodes.label: Frontier
nodes.rationale: The open, unblocked, unclaimed child tickets — the edge of the known. Blocking uses the tracker's native dependency relationship so the frontier renders visually in the tracker's own UI without opening the map.

nodes.label: Predictability
nodes.rationale: The root virtue of skill design: a skill exists to wrangle determinism out of a stochastic system. Predictability means the agent takes the same process every run, not the same output — a brainstorming skill should predictably diverge. Cost and maintainability are symptoms of it, not rivals.

nodes.label: Leading Word
nodes.rationale: A compact pretrained concept (Leitwort) the agent thinks with while running a skill — e.g. lesson, fog of war, tracer bullets, proximal zone of development. It encodes a behavioural principle in minimal tokens by recruiting priors the model already holds; a made-up word recruits no priors, so you pay in definition tokens what a pretrained word gives free. Serves predictability twice: anchoring execution in the body and invocation in the description.

nodes.label: Progressive Disclosure
nodes.rationale: Moving reference down the information hierarchy — out of SKILL.md and behind a context pointer — to protect legibility of the top, not primarily to save tokens. Licensed by branching: disclose what only some branches need, inline what every path needs. If a pointer fires unreliably on must-have material, sharpen its wording before inlining.

nodes.label: Premature Completion
nodes.rationale: Failure mode: ending a step before it is genuinely done because attention slips to being done. A tug-of-war between visible post-completion steps (the pull) and completion-criterion clarity (the resistance). Defence in order: sharpen the bound first (cheap, local); only hide later steps across a real context boundary when the criterion is irreducibly fuzzy and the rush is observed.

nodes.label: Router Skill
nodes.rationale: A user-invoked skill that points at your other user-invoked skills — naming each and when to reach for it — so the human remembers one skill instead of many. It can only hint, never fire them, because user-invoked skills have no description. The cure for cognitive load when user-invoked skills multiply.

nodes.label: Writing Great Skills Skill
nodes.rationale: Reference for writing predictable skills: pick model- vs user-invocation by whether the agent must reach the skill on its own; arrange content on the information hierarchy ladder (in-skill steps, in-skill reference, external reference); split by invocation or sequence only when the cut earns its load; prune duplication, sediment, sprawl, and no-ops sentence by sentence.

nodes.label: Ask Matt Router Skill
nodes.rationale: A router over the skills in this repo: you don't remember every skill, so ask. Defines flows as paths through the skills — one main flow, two on-ramps, standalone skills, and a vocabulary layer running underneath.

nodes.label: Main Flow: Idea to Ship
nodes.rationale: grill-with-docs → (optional prototype detour bridged by /handoff) → to-prd → to-issues → implement (driving /tdd internally, closing with /code-review). Steps 1-3 stay in one unbroken context window so grilling, PRD, and issues build on the same thinking; each /implement starts fresh per issue.

nodes.label: Smart Zone
nodes.rationale: The context window (~120k tokens on state-of-the-art models) within which the model still reasons sharply. If a session approaches it before /to-issues, don't push on degraded — /handoff and continue in a fresh thread.

nodes.label: Two-Axis Review (Standards + Spec)
nodes.rationale: Standards (does the code follow the repo's documented standards?) and Spec (does it match what the issue/PRD asked?) run as parallel sub-agents so they don't pollute each other's context, and findings are never merged or reranked — a change can pass one axis and fail the other, and reporting them separately stops one axis from masking the other.

nodes.label: Fowler Smell Baseline
nodes.rationale: A fixed set of twelve Fowler code smells (Mysterious Name, Duplicated Code, Feature Envy, Data Clumps, Primitive Obsession, Repeated Switches, Shotgun Surgery, Divergent Change, Speculative Generality, Message Chains, Middle Man, Refused Bequest) applied even when a repo documents nothing. The repo overrides the baseline; every smell is a labelled judgement call, never a hard violation; skip anything tooling already enforces.

nodes.label: Deepening Guide
nodes.rationale: How to deepen a cluster of shallow modules safely: classify each dependency (in-process, local-substitutable, remote-but-owned, true external) because the category determines how the deepened module is tested across its seam.

nodes.label: Ports and Adapters
nodes.rationale: For dependencies across a network boundary: define a port (interface) at the seam; the deep module owns the logic and the transport is injected as an adapter — in-memory for tests, HTTP/gRPC/queue for production — so the logic sits in one deep module even though it's deployed across a network. Third-party externals get a mock adapter instead.

nodes.label: Replace, Don't Layer (Testing Strategy)
nodes.rationale: Old unit tests on shallow modules become waste once tests exist at the deepened module's interface — delete them. New tests assert observable outcomes through the interface and survive internal refactors; a test that must change when the implementation changes is testing past the interface.

nodes.label: Design It Twice
nodes.rationale: Your first idea is unlikely to be the best (Ousterhout). Spawn 3+ parallel sub-agents, each producing a radically different interface under a different design constraint (minimize interface, maximize flexibility, optimize for the common caller, ports-and-adapters), then compare on depth, locality, and seam placement — and give an opinionated recommendation, not a menu.

nodes.label: Deep Module
nodes.rationale: A lot of behaviour behind a small interface at a clean seam: depth is leverage at the interface, producing leverage for callers and locality for maintainers. Ousterhout's depth-as-line-ratio framing is explicitly rejected because it rewards padding the implementation. The deletion test: if deleting the module makes complexity reappear across N callers, it was earning its keep.

nodes.label: Seam
nodes.author: Michael Feathers
nodes.rationale: A place where you can alter behaviour without editing in that place — the location where a module's interface lives (term from Michael Feathers; 'boundary' is avoided as overloaded with DDD). One adapter means a hypothetical seam, two adapters means a real one: don't introduce a seam unless something actually varies across it.

nodes.label: The Interface Is the Test Surface
nodes.rationale: Callers and tests cross the same seam; if you want to test past the interface, the module is probably the wrong shape. Testability follows from interface design: accept dependencies rather than create them, return results rather than produce side effects, keep the surface small.
nodes.norm_label: the interface is the test surface

nodes.label: Diagnosing Bugs Skill
nodes.rationale: Six-phase discipline for hard bugs: build a feedback loop, reproduce and minimise, generate 3-5 falsifiable ranked hypotheses (single-hypothesis generation anchors on the first plausible idea), instrument one variable at a time with tagged logs, fix with a regression test written before the fix, then cleanup and post-mortem asking what would have prevented the bug.

nodes.label: Architecture Decision Record (ADR)
nodes.rationale: Sequentially numbered files in docs/adr/, as short as a single paragraph — the value is recording that a decision was made and why. Offered only when all three hold: hard to reverse, surprising without context, and the result of a real trade-off. Explicit no-s and rejected alternatives are as valuable as the yes-s; they stop the next engineer from 'fixing' something deliberate.

nodes.label: CONTEXT.md Glossary
nodes.rationale: An opinionated glossary of project-specific terms (each with tight one-to-two-sentence definitions and Avoid-aliases), totally devoid of implementation details — a glossary and nothing else. Single-context repos have one root CONTEXT.md; multi-context repos add a CONTEXT-MAP.md listing contexts and their relationships. Files are created lazily, when the first term is resolved.

nodes.label: Domain Modeling Skill
nodes.rationale: The active discipline of building the domain model: challenge terms against the glossary, sharpen fuzzy language, stress-test relationships with concrete edge-case scenarios, cross-reference claims with code, and update CONTEXT.md inline as terms resolve. Merely reading CONTEXT.md for vocabulary is not this skill.

nodes.label: Grilling Skill
nodes.rationale: One question at a time because asking multiple questions at once is bewildering; codebase-answerable questions are explored instead of asked; the plan is not enacted until shared understanding is confirmed.

nodes.label: Handoff Document
nodes.rationale: Saved to the OS temp directory, not the workspace; references existing artifacts (PRDs, ADRs, issues) by path instead of duplicating them; sensitive information is redacted; includes a suggested-skills section for the next agent.

nodes.label: Deepening Opportunity
nodes.rationale: Refactors that turn shallow modules into deep ones; the aim is testability and AI-navigability, not abstract cleanliness.

nodes.label: Architecture Review HTML Report Format
nodes.rationale: Single self-contained HTML file in the OS temp directory (nothing lands in the repo); Tailwind and Mermaid from CDNs; Mermaid for graph-shaped diagrams, hand-built divs/SVG for editorial visuals to avoid a generic look.

nodes.label: Before/After Diagram Patterns
nodes.rationale: Mermaid graphs for call flow, hand-built boxes-and-arrows when layout fights Mermaid, cross-sections for layered shallowness, mass diagrams for interface-vs-implementation surface area, call-graph collapse. Variety across candidates is deliberate; if a diagram needs a paragraph to be understood, redraw it.

nodes.label: Codebase Design Glossary Vocabulary
nodes.rationale: Use exactly: module, interface, implementation, depth, deep, shallow, seam, adapter, leverage, locality. Never substitute component/service/API/boundary/layer — terms not in the glossary don't earn their place.

nodes.label: Throwaway Code That Answers a Question
nodes.rationale: The question decides the shape (logic vs UI branch); no persistence, no tests, no polish; the answer is the only durable artifact — the prototype is deleted or absorbed when done.
nodes.norm_label: throwaway code that answers a question

nodes.label: Logic Prototype
nodes.rationale: A tiny interactive TUI that lets the user drive a state model by hand, for questions about business logic, state transitions, or data shape that only feel wrong when pushed through real cases.

nodes.label: UI Prototype
nodes.rationale: Several radically different (structurally different, not colour-tweaked) UI variants on a single route gated by a ?variant= URL param; prefer mounting inside an existing page because a route in a vacuum makes every variant look fine.

nodes.label: Floating Variant Switcher
nodes.rationale: Fixed bottom-centre bar cycling variants via URL search param so variants are shareable and reload-stable; visually distinct from the design under evaluation; hidden in production builds so a stray merge can't ship it.

nodes.label: Research Skill
nodes.rationale: Runs as a background agent so the main session keeps working while it reads.

nodes.label: Primary Sources
nodes.rationale: Official docs, source code, specs, first-party APIs — not secondary write-ups; every claim is followed back to the source that owns it and cited in the findings file.

nodes.label: Setup Matt Pocock Skills
nodes.rationale: Prompt-driven, not a deterministic script: explore the repo, present findings, walk the user through issue-tracker / triage-label / domain-doc decisions one at a time, confirm, then write docs/agents/*.md and an Agent skills block into whichever of CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md already exists.

nodes.label: GitHub Issue Tracker Convention
nodes.rationale: All operations via the gh CLI; external PRs can optionally be a triage surface (filtered by authorAssociation); wayfinding uses native sub-issues and issue dependencies with body-line fallbacks.

nodes.label: GitLab Issue Tracker Convention
nodes.rationale: All operations via the glab CLI; comments are 'notes', PRs are 'merge requests'; native blocking links are Premium/Ultimate-only so a Blocked-by body line is the free-tier fallback.

nodes.label: Local Markdown Issue Tracker Convention
nodes.rationale: Issues and PRDs live as markdown files under .scratch/<feature>/ — good for solo projects or repos without a remote; triage state is a Status: line, blocking is a Blocked-by line.

nodes.label: Triage Label Vocabulary
nodes.rationale: Five canonical roles (needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix) mapped to the repo's actual label strings so skills apply existing labels instead of creating duplicates.

nodes.label: Domain Docs Consumption Rules
nodes.rationale: Skills read CONTEXT.md (or CONTEXT-MAP.md for multi-context repos) and docs/adr/ before exploring; missing files are ignored silently because /domain-modeling creates them lazily when terms or decisions actually get resolved.

nodes.label: CONTEXT.md Domain Glossary
nodes.rationale: Canonical domain vocabulary; outputs must use its terms and not drift to synonyms the glossary avoids; a missing term signals either invented language or a real gap for /domain-modeling.

nodes.label: Architecture Decision Records (docs/adr)
nodes.rationale: Record decisions that skills should not re-litigate; conflicts must be surfaced explicitly ('Contradicts ADR-0007 — but worth reopening because…') rather than silently overridden.

nodes.label: Vertical Slice / Tracer Bullet
nodes.rationale: One test → one implementation → repeat, each test responding to what the last cycle taught; bulk-writing all tests first (horizontal slicing) verifies imagined behavior and commits to test structure before understanding the implementation.

nodes.label: Integration-Style Test
nodes.rationale: Tests observable behavior through the public API only, describes WHAT not HOW, and survives internal refactors.

nodes.label: Implementation-Detail Test (Anti-Pattern)
nodes.rationale: Coupled to internal structure: mocks internal collaborators, asserts call counts, or verifies through side channels like direct DB queries; the tell is that it breaks on refactor without a behavior change.

nodes.label: Tautological Test (Anti-Pattern)
nodes.rationale: The expected value is recomputed the way the code computes it, so the test passes by construction; expected values must come from an independent source of truth — a known-good literal, worked example, or spec.

nodes.label: Mock at System Boundaries
nodes.rationale: Mock only external APIs, time/randomness, sometimes databases and filesystem; never your own classes or internal collaborators — anything you control should be exercised for real.

nodes.label: Dependency Injection
nodes.rationale: Pass external dependencies in rather than constructing them internally, so boundary mocks can be substituted in tests.

nodes.label: SDK-Style Interface
nodes.rationale: One specific function per external operation instead of a generic fetcher, so each mock returns one shape, test setup has no conditional logic, and each endpoint gets its own type safety.

nodes.label: GLOSSARY.md Format
nodes.rationale: A record of compressed knowledge, not a dictionary to learn from: terms are added only once the user demonstrably understands them, definitions are opinionated with avoided aliases, and glossary terms are reused inside other definitions to compound compression.

nodes.label: Learning Record
nodes.rationale: The teaching equivalent of ADRs: sequentially numbered files capturing demonstrated understanding, disclosed prior knowledge, corrected misconceptions, and mission shifts — decision-grade insights, not a session journal; superseded records are marked, not deleted, because the history of understanding is itself signal.

nodes.label: Zone of Proximal Development
nodes.rationale: Learning records set the floor of what is known and MISSION.md's out-of-scope list bounds it, together determining what to teach next.

nodes.label: MISSION.md Format
nodes.rationale: Captures the concrete real-world reason the user is learning; one mission per workspace, concrete over abstract ('run a half marathon by October' beats 'get fitter'), revised when reality shifts, and kept under a screen so it stays a compass rather than a plan.

nodes.label: Fluency vs Storage Strength
nodes.rationale: Fluency gives an illusory sense of mastery; storage strength (long-term retention via retrieval practice, spacing, interleaving) is the real goal of lessons.

nodes.label: Learning Mission
nodes.rationale: The reason the user wants to learn; without it knowledge acquisition is ungrounded, lessons feel abstract, and there is no way to judge what comes next.

nodes.label: RESOURCES.md Format
nodes.rationale: High-trust sources only, every entry annotated, grouped Knowledge/Wisdom, gaps surfaced explicitly, pruned ruthlessly — five sharp sources beat thirty mediocre ones.

nodes.label: Testing Seam
nodes.rationale: Prefer existing seams at the highest point possible; the fewer seams across the codebase the better — the ideal number is one.

nodes.label: PRD Template
nodes.rationale: Problem/Solution/User Stories/Implementation Decisions/Testing Decisions/Out of Scope; no file paths or code snippets because they go stale — except decision-rich prototype snippets.

nodes.label: Agent Brief
nodes.rationale: Durability over precision: describe interfaces, types, and behavioral contracts, never file paths or line numbers, because the codebase changes while the issue sits in ready-for-agent; behavioral not procedural; complete testable acceptance criteria; explicit scope boundaries.

nodes.label: Out-of-Scope Knowledge Base
nodes.rationale: One file per concept for institutional memory and deduplication of rejected feature requests; only rejected enhancements are recorded — recording already-implemented features would poison the dedup checks with false rejections.

nodes.label: Shared Map
nodes.rationale: A single issue labelled wayfinder:map; an index, not a store — each decision lives in exactly one place (its ticket), the map only gists and links.

nodes.label: Wayfinder Ticket Types
nodes.rationale: Four types — research, prototype, grilling (the default), task — each sized to one 100K-token agent session.

nodes.label: Skill-Writing Glossary
nodes.rationale: The domain model for what makes a skill great, grouped by four axes: Invocation, Information Hierarchy, Steering, Pruning — each failure mode lives beside the lever that cures it.

nodes.label: Predictability
nodes.rationale: The root virtue: a skill exists to wrangle determinism out of a stochastic system — same process every run, not same output; cost and maintainability are symptoms of it, not rivals.

nodes.label: Context Load
nodes.rationale: The always-loaded description cost a model-invoked skill imposes on the context window; the brake on splitting into more model-invoked skills.

nodes.label: Cognitive Load
nodes.rationale: What the human must remember for user-invoked skills; not a cost to minimise — it is the price of human agency, spent where human judgement matters.

nodes.label: Information Hierarchy
nodes.rationale: A skill's content ranked by immediacy of need: in-file steps, in-file reference, disclosed reference behind a context pointer; push too little down and the top bloats, push too much and needed material hides.

nodes.label: Completion Criterion
nodes.rationale: Two lever properties: clarity resists premature completion; demand sets legwork — the strongest criteria are both checkable and exhaustive.

nodes.label: Issue Before Implementation Rule
nodes.rationale: No code, config, or infra change starts without a GitHub issue describing the work; reference an existing issue instead of duplicating, noting the number in branch name or PR.

nodes.label: Not-the-Next.js-You-Know Warning
nodes.rationale: This repo's Next.js has breaking changes versus training data — agents must read the bundled guides in node_modules/next/dist/docs/ before writing code and heed deprecation notices.

nodes.label: Gitflow Workflow
nodes.rationale: main is release-only; develop is the integration branch; feature/<name> branches cut from develop; hotfixes from main merge to both main and develop.

nodes.label: Graphify Knowledge Graph Convention
nodes.rationale: For codebase questions, run graphify query/path/explain first — a scoped subgraph is usually much smaller than GRAPH_REPORT.md or raw grep; run graphify update after modifying code.

nodes.label: Kitchen Disco Theme
nodes.rationale: 'Kitchen disco at grandma's 80th' — drenched deep dance-floor green IS the brand surface; dark theme by design because the audience is mid-scroll on a phone at night and the product output is vertical video on a dark stage.

nodes.label: OKLCH Color Tokens
nodes.rationale: OKLCH only, never hex; pink --go is reserved for the single primary action per screen; butter marks 'where you are'; extend the ramp at hue ~150; defined in src/app/globals.css and mapped to Tailwind via @theme inline.

nodes.label: Studio Wizard Pattern
nodes.rationale: One raised surface shell, step pills in the header (butter = current, check = done), one obvious next action per step; cards only where content is truly selectable.

nodes.label: Motion System
nodes.rationale: Keyframes (bob, sway, hip, disco-spin, marquee, etc.) with snappy exponential ease; every animation collapses under prefers-reduced-motion — the dancer freezes into a pose.

nodes.label: DancingGrandma Product Strategy
nodes.rationale: Photo in, hilarious video out, share it in the group chat; brand is cheeky, festive, family-warm — a kitchen disco at a family party, never mean or ironic-cold.

nodes.label: Three Taps to Joy
nodes.rationale: Users arrive on mobile mid-scroll with 30 seconds of attention and zero tolerance for setup friction — photo, dance, go; every screen has exactly one obvious next action.

nodes.label: Affectionate Absurdity
nodes.rationale: We laugh with grandma, never at her — grandma is always the hero, never the punchline; punching-down meme sites are an explicit anti-reference.

nodes.label: The Demo IS the Pitch
nodes.rationale: Show a before/after (photo to dancing video) above the fold — don't explain what a paragraph can't sell but a clip can.

nodes.label: Made for the Group Chat
nodes.rationale: Every output state is one tap from share; vertical video is a first-class citizen.

nodes.label: Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA)
nodes.rationale: Body text >=4.5:1, large display >=3:1, full prefers-reduced-motion alternatives, keyboard-navigable uploads, video previews always have controls and never autoplay with sound.

nodes.label: Mocked Generation MVP
nodes.rationale: Generation is mocked while the video-model research lands, but the upload/pick/generate/share flow is real end-to-end.

nodes.label: Wan 2.2 Animate 14B via fal.ai
nodes.rationale: The production pipeline is designed around Wan 2.2 Animate 14B (Apache 2.0) served via fal.ai.

nodes.label: Kling 2.6 Motion Control
nodes.rationale: A selectable alternative video engine alongside Wan 2.2 Animate.

nodes.label: Video Engine Registry (src/lib/engines.ts)
nodes.rationale: The single place to add or flip video engines.

nodes.label: Domain Docs Convention
nodes.rationale: Read CONTEXT.md (or CONTEXT-MAP.md per context) and docs/adr/ before exploring; if absent, proceed silently — /domain-modeling creates them lazily when terms actually get resolved.

nodes.label: CONTEXT.md Domain Glossary
nodes.rationale: Outputs must use terms as defined in CONTEXT.md and not drift to avoided synonyms; a missing concept signals either invented language or a real gap for /domain-modeling.

nodes.label: Architecture Decision Records
nodes.rationale: ADRs in docs/adr/ scope decisions; outputs contradicting an ADR must surface the conflict explicitly rather than silently overriding.

nodes.label: GitHub Issue Tracker Convention
nodes.rationale: Issues and PRDs live as GitHub issues operated via the gh CLI; 'publish to the issue tracker' means create a GitHub issue.

nodes.label: PRs as Triage Surface
nodes.rationale: External PRs run through the same labels and states as issues; discovery keeps only authorAssociation CONTRIBUTOR/FIRST_TIME_CONTRIBUTOR/NONE — a collaborator's in-flight PR is not triage work.

nodes.label: Triage Label Mapping
nodes.rationale: Maps the five canonical triage roles (needs-triage, needs-info, ready-for-agent, ready-for-human, wontfix) to this repo's actual label strings — identical 1:1 here.

nodes.label: Sora 2 Video Infrastructure (Bicep)
nodes.rationale: Subscription-scoped main.bicep creates the resource group then the stack (resources.bicep): AI Foundry account, sora-2 GlobalStandard deployment, storage account with a videos blob container.

nodes.label: Sora 2 Deployment & Videos API
nodes.rationale: Verified end-to-end with sora-2 (2025-12-08): uses the *.openai.azure.com endpoint (not the default cognitiveservices one) and the OpenAI-style /openai/v1/videos API with api-version=preview; size and seconds are strings.

nodes.label: NamePrefix + Scope-Hash Naming
nodes.rationale: All resource names derive from namePrefix plus a hash of the target scope so the whole stack clones to another RG or subscription without collisions; nothing is hard-coded to a subscription.

nodes.label: Public Repo Secret Hygiene
nodes.rationale: Public repo: the resource group and subscription are never committed — provided at deploy time via the gitignored infra/main.bicepparam (copied from main.bicepparam.example) or CLI parameters.

nodes.label: Bundled Trending-Dance Reference Clips
nodes.rationale: Every curated dance ships with a reference video (griddy, renegade, macarena, disco, woah) so each card renders on a fresh clone with no setup; clips carry their own audio, transcoded to engine-friendly H.264/AAC MP4 (<=15s, <=720px wide).

nodes.label: Clip Import Pipeline (yt-dlp + ffmpeg)
nodes.rationale: Clips were fetched with the in-app import pipeline (src/lib/import-clip.ts) from public dance clips, transcoded with the audio track preserved; adding a dance means dropping <id>.mp4 here plus a matching DANCES entry in src/components/Studio.tsx.

nodes.label: Bundled Clip Licensing Tradeoff
nodes.rationale: The committed clips are other creators' content published in a public repo; the owner accepted that tradeoff for a demo — anything shipped more broadly should use licensed/owned footage or keep clips local via .gitignore.
